title: Moral Use of the Law — Part I
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-28-23 - Moral Use of the Law (part I) - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Series Context and Review
subpoints: 22-part series on the moral law using the Westminster Larger Catechism
Team teaching: Pastor Lyro, Elder Scott, Andrew, Isaiah
Review: the moral law is written in every human heart and revealed in creation
Last week: combating legalism — we cannot relate to God through law-keeping

outline.title: The Law's Usefulness for All People (WLC Q. 95)
subpoints: Three groups: all men, the unregenerate, the regenerate
Four ways the law is useful to all people:
1. As a revelation of truth concerning God — his holy nature and will
Leviticus: 'Be holy, for I am holy' — the law reveals the character of God
2. As a revelation of humanity's moral obligation to God
Micah 6:8; Matthew 22 — all humanity is obligated to God, not just Christians
3. As a means of convincing people of their utterly sinful condition
Romans 7: the law drove Paul to see he couldn't keep it
4. As help toward a right estimate of Christ's matchless character

outline.title: The Law's Usefulness for the Unregenerate (WLC Q. 96)
subpoints: The unregenerate are also commanded to follow the law of God
1 Timothy 1:9-10: the law is given to the unregenerate specifically
The law awakens the conscience to flee from the wrath to come
The moral law drives sinners to Christ for salvation — the gospel cannot be presented without it
The 'God loves you and has a wonderful plan' gospel misses this
Romans 1-3: Paul confronts both Jew and Gentile with the law before the 'but God' of 3:21
The law leaves the unregenerate without excuse (Romans 1:20, 2:15)

outline.title: The Law's Usefulness for the Regenerate (WLC Q. 97)
subpoints: The regenerate are not under the law as a covenant of works — neither justified nor condemned by it
The law shows believers how much they are bound to Christ
The law provokes thankfulness — the more we see we can't keep it, the more grateful we are for Christ
The law is the believer's standard of obedience — the rule of life
Titus 2:11-14: the grace of God teaches us how to live godly, as a response to grace
Psalm 19: we can know what God wants us to do by looking at his law

outline.title: Debate on Natural Law vs. Biblical Law in the Public Square
subpoints: Two-kingdoms theology (Westminster Seminary CA): use natural law in public discourse, not Scripture
But ultimately all moral argument comes back to the law of God
The Bible is useful for everyone, not just Christians — it is the authoritative standard
Without appealing to God's law, abortion can only be argued pragmatically — no absolute ground

summary:
In the third lesson of the Moral Law series, Pastor Tito Lyro returns to the Larger Catechism's analysis of the law's usefulness (WLC Q. 95-97), now working through each group in detail. He notes that the series will ultimately be 22 lessons and will touch on every area of Christian life including parenting, living in society, and evangelism, since the moral law — summarized in the Ten Commandments and then in the two great commandments — touches everything.

For all humanity (Q. 95), the law is useful in four ways: as a revelation of God's holy nature and will; as a revelation of humanity's moral obligation to God; as a means of confronting all people with their inability to meet God's standard; and as a means of generating appreciation for Christ's perfect obedience. For the unregenerate (Q. 96), the law serves to awaken conscience and drive sinners either to Christ or to condemnation. Pastor Lyro argues forcefully that the gospel cannot be faithfully presented without the moral law — this is why Paul spends three chapters in Romans confronting Jew and Gentile with sin before arriving at the gospel declaration in 3:21.

For the regenerate (Q. 97), the law serves as the rule of obedience and a prompt for gratitude. Believers are not under the law as a covenant of works — they are neither justified nor condemned by it — but they use it to understand what God requires and to express their thankfulness for grace. The lesson engages a current debate within Reformed circles on whether natural law or biblical law should be used in public discourse, concluding that all genuine moral argument ultimately rests on the law of God.
transcript:
## Series Context and Review

The birthday candy basket seems to have been visited quite a bit between celebrations because it has really gone down. So we have to replenish it. But just so people know, it is for the birthday celebrations, not for dessert throughout the month.

Just before we start a lesson — someone asked about our new logo. The thing in the middle is the burning bush. The burning bush was adopted as a symbol of Presbyterianism by Irish Presbyterians a couple of centuries ago. And eventually became the symbol of English-speaking Presbyterianism and now tends to be the symbol of Presbyterianism all over the world. So that's the burning bush — our burning bush. To our knowledge there's no other design of the burning bush like that. That's our proprietary burning bush.

Just a couple of dates. Jerry had surgery on Friday. Jerry called in. He's doing well. He went home same day and things are going well for him. Memorial was in the hospital over the last few days, but she's back home trying to figure out what she needs and what insurance can cover and what we as a church can help her. So wait for news on that.

All right, we are talking — we've been going through a series on the law of God, the moral law specifically, the law that God has written in our hearts. But also the law of God that reveals itself in creation. And we are using the Larger Catechism to help us in this effort of learning about the moral law of God. It's going to be a 22-part series. But through it, we're going to be talking about parenting, living in society, evangelism, and so on. Because as the moral law is summarized in the Ten Commandments and then further summarized in the two great commandments of loving God with all every ounce of your being and loving others as yourself, you see that it touches pretty much every single area of life.

So I've been teaching the first few lessons, but Scott also is going to be involved, and Andrew and Isaiah — all involved in the teaching of this series. If everything goes as planned, December 7th will be our last lesson on this series.

We've been looking at the fact that the moral law is useful. And we saw last week that when the catechism talks about it being useful, it's not addressing the traditional reformed approach of looking at the three uses of the law. It's a bigger approach to this idea of the law. God in his goodness and mercy wrote the moral law in the heart of all of humanity — every last person who has existed, who exists today, and who comes to existence in the future — has the moral law of God written in their heart. And also in his goodness, he has revealed that law in creation.

Last week we spent some time fighting against the error of legalism — how some people tried to look at their own ability to fulfill the things that God commands us to do in the Bible as the means by which they're going to relate to God. And when they fail, then they create their own standards, their own set of rules, thinking if I meet those, then I'm okay with God. And we saw that that's never possible. The law of God is not useful for your justification. You cannot have a relationship with God based on your obedience to the moral law.

## The Moral Law Is Useful for Three Groups

Today we're going to study how the moral law is useful. And it is interesting that the catechism also says it's useful for three groups of people. The first group is all people. The second group is the unregenerate people — unbelievers. And the third group is the regenerate people, the believers in the Church of Jesus Christ. And then we're going to see how to each one of these groups the law is useful.

You can go to bpc.org — which is our denominational website — click on beliefs, and you have access to the whole Larger Catechism either in HTML or as a PDF.

## The Law Is Useful for All People (WLC Q. 95)

So the moral law is useful to all people, and it's useful to all people in four ways. Here is question 95 of the Westminster Larger Catechism: "Of what use is the moral law to all men? The moral law is of use to all men to inform them of the holy nature and will of God and of their duty binding them to walk accordingly; to convince them of their disability to keep it and of the sinful pollution of their nature, hearts and lives; to humble them in the sense of their sin and misery and thereby help them to a clearer sight of the need they have of Christ and of the perfection of his obedience."

Can you name some of the ways that the law of God is helpful to all people just by reading the question? First: to inform one of the holy nature and will of God. Second: to inform one of their duty. Third: disability to keep it and the pollution of sin. Fourth: to humble them and help them see their need of Christ.

The first thing this question tells us is that the law of God is useful to all people as a revelation of truth concerning God. The moral law is an expression of the holy nature and will of God. That's the refrain of the book of Leviticus — time and again: "Be holy, for I am holy." God doesn't say, "You know, do these things because all these benefits will come to you, or because life in society is going to work better." He says: "Do these things because I'm holy — it reveals my nature. It reveals who I am." And the basis for Peter's call to holiness is the holiness of God revealed in the law: in 1 Peter he says we have to be holy and then quotes Leviticus as the reason — because God is holy.

Secondly, the law is useful for all people because it is a revelation of truth concerning humanity's moral obligation to God. Not only for the church, but the entirety of humanity — every last human being who ever lived — has an obligation toward God. Can you think of a passage that tells us that?

Micah 6:8: "He has shown you, O man, what is good, and what does the Lord require of you but to do justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God?" Yes. And the interaction of Jesus with the rich young ruler — when the rich and ruler says, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Jesus directs him to the moral law. And in Matthew 22 when he's asked what are the most important commandments, he says: "Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and love your neighbor as yourself — in these consists all the law and the prophets." So the entire Old Testament can be summarized in these two things. And he says that's what everyone needs to do — indicating the moral obligation that every last human has toward God.

The third thing is that the law of God is useful for all people as a means of convincing people of their utterly sinful condition by nature. It does that by placing before humanity an absolute standard. And the more they try to keep it, the more evident it becomes that they can't. One of the best ways to show somebody that they really can't obey God as God requires is to say: here are the Ten Commandments. I want you to keep a log, and just for five days, we'll meet again in five days, and the log should be blank because the log should show every time you broke one of those commandments. If they're honest, the log is not going to be blank — it's going to be very full. So the law shows all humanity how they're unable to achieve or meet God's call for moral life in their hearts. We see that struggle that Paul had in Romans chapter 7, where it was the law of God that drove him to see that he couldn't keep the law of God.

And fourthly, the law of God is helpful to all people because it is helpful toward a right estimate of the matchless character of Christ. We try to keep the law and we can't. Then we see in the Bible that Christ kept it perfectly. It helps us think very highly of Christ and also of our need of him, and we cling to him more closely.

## The Law Is Useful for the Unregenerate (WLC Q. 96)

The moral law is also useful to unregenerate people — not just people in general, but the unbeliever out there. Question 96: "What particular use is there of the moral law to unregenerate men? The moral law is of use to unregenerate men to awaken their conscience to flee from the wrath to come and to drive them to Christ; or upon their continuance in their estate and way of sin, to leave them inexcusable and under the curse thereof."

So there is great use of the moral law for the unregenerate. Do you realize that the unregenerate are also commanded to follow the law of God? That's not an option given to them. You can see that in 1 Timothy 1:9-10 where Paul actually says the law is given to the unregenerate specifically.

If you are a law-abiding citizen you never have any interaction with the law — at least the enforcement of it. That's why some of the most law-abiding drivers are people who are afraid of being pulled over. So as far as traffic laws go, they tend to be very careful and they have no interaction with the effects of the law. The unregenerate are always hitting against the boundaries of the law and having all kinds of interaction with it.

The moral law is an instrument that God uses to drive sinners to Christ for salvation. And that means that you cannot present the gospel apart from the moral law. Are you with me on that one? "God loves you and has a wonderful plan for your life" doesn't really portray what the Bible teaches concerning the presentation of the gospel. Remember that Romans is a presentation of the gospel. And Paul spends chapters 1 through 3 confronting both Jew and Gentile with the law of God before he gets to 3:21 with the "but now." It makes sense — so he cannot really proclaim the gospel apart from the moral law of God, because the moral law is what points people to Christ.

And the moral law leaves the unregenerate without excuses. If you combine Romans 1:20, which says that they can see God in creation, with Romans 2:15, which says that God not only revealed himself in creation but wrote the law in their hearts — then there is no one who can stand before God and say, "I deserve an excuse here because I wasn't really aware of what was going on." The moral law also leaves unregenerate men without excuse.

## The Law Is Useful for the Regenerate (WLC Q. 97)

And lastly, the moral law is useful to the regenerate people — that's us. Question 97: "What special use is there of the moral law to the regenerate? Although they that are regenerate and believing in Christ be delivered from the moral law as a covenant of works, so as thereby they are neither justified nor condemned, yet besides the general uses thereof common to them with all men, it is of special use to show them how much they are bound to Christ for his fulfilling it and enduring the curse thereof in their stead and for their good, and thereby to provoke them to more thankfulness and to express the same in their greater care to conform themselves thereunto as the rule of their obedience."

So what is this question teaching? It's teaching that the regenerate are not under the law as a covenant of works, like the unregenerate are. You and I who believe in Jesus — we're not relating to God through the covenant of works. The unbeliever who does not have Christ is relating to God based on the covenant of works — based on their obedience. We are not, because Christ has obeyed God on our behalf.

This also teaches that the moral law is the believer's standard of obedience — this is what we use to figure out if we're obeying God or not. Not in order to earn eternal life by obeying it, but in order to express gratitude to God for the free grace of salvation. Titus 2:11-14 is a key passage: "The grace of God has appeared to all men, teaching us to deny ungodliness and worldly lusts, and to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present age." The necessary reaction to the grace of God is obedience. But it is a response to grace, not in order to gain grace.

The Bible doesn't have a category for disobedient Christians, because the necessary consequence of the grace of God is obedience to the word of God. And also, the moral law of God is useful to the regenerate as a guide for sanctification. We see that in Psalm 19 — in the second half — God teaches there that we can know what he wants us to do by looking at his law. We don't have to come up with a system — it's right here.

## Debate: Natural Law vs. Biblical Law

You see that in using the law — we have to talk to people knowing that the Bible is not like a standard for us as Christians and a different standard for everybody else out there. There's one authoritative standard — the moral law of God — and we interact with people on that basis. 

Now a lot of Catholic theologians through the years have written about the idea of only using natural law to talk to people in the public square. And in the Protestant reformed world, it came out of Westminster Seminary in California with the idea labeled two-kingdoms teaching — that says there are two kingdoms: the church, and God speaks to the church through this covenantal book that we call the Bible; and the state. The Bible wasn't given to the state, so in the public arena you should use just what's revealed in nature, in logic, and in common sense to make points. The main proponent is Meredith Kline.

The problem with that is: the only reason abortion is wrong is because God said so. There's no other ultimate reason. You can argue for humanitarian reasons, pragmatic reasons, and so on — but ultimately the taking of a life is only wrong because God in his law said this is wrong. It makes sense — there is no way to make a truly moral argument without somehow coming back to the law of God.

## Conclusion

So the Lord Jesus Christ kept the moral law for us. That's what we have to just hold on to, so we don't fear the condemnation of the law. We don't live under that. The law helps us to become more like our Lord — that's the main use for us as regenerate people. The law helps us to become more like Jesus Christ by giving us a pattern to follow. Not a pattern that we can pick and choose from, but a pattern to which we must conform if we want to become more like Jesus Christ.

Next time we will consider how to interpret the Ten Commandments, getting more specific. Now we're going to look at the Ten Commandments as the summary of the moral law and how we should interpret them.

Any questions before we close? All right, so let's pray. Father in heaven, thank you that you are a good God. We thank you that you reveal yourself to us in creation, in the law written in our hearts, but ultimately in Jesus Christ. We thank you for bringing us to yourself through him. Help us to grow in him in response to the grace that's been so lavishly poured out upon us. In Jesus' name, amen.
